title: Rain Shadow Effect Visualizer
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive MicroSim demonstrating how mountain ranges create wet and dry climate zones through adiabatic cooling and warming.

educational.topic: Rain Shadow Effect and Biome Distribution
learningObjectives: Explain how mountain ranges create wet and dry climate zones through the rain shadow effect
Describe the process of adiabatic cooling and warming as air moves over terrain
Predict how mountain height affects precipitation patterns and biome distribution
Connect rain shadow effects to real-world geography and ecosystem distribution
prerequisites: Basic understanding of the water cycle
Familiarity with biomes and climate zones
Understanding of how temperature affects air moisture capacity

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Animated cross-section with parameter manipulation and real-world examples
keyQuestions: Why is the leeward side of a mountain range drier than the windward side?
How does mountain height affect the severity of the rain shadow?
What happens to temperature and humidity as air rises over a mountain?
How does the rain shadow effect influence where biomes are located?
Why are many of the world's deserts located in rain shadows?
commonMisconceptions: Mountains block rain clouds from passing over them like a wall
The leeward side is dry because rain clouds run out of water, rather than because of adiabatic warming
Rain shadow effects are the same regardless of mountain height
assessmentOpportunities: Students predict vegetation changes at different mountain heights before testing
Students compare simulation results to real mountain range data
Students explain the step-by-step process of rain shadow formation in their own words
